lines.ka: კარგად ვარ, გმადლობ. შენ როგორ ხარ?
lines.en: I'm fine, thanks. How are you?
Yes, the coffee here is great.

note: Between friends and peers Georgians use the informal შენ; მაგარია (literally 'it's strong') is everyday slang for 'it's great'.

lines.ka: გამარჯობა! მე თამარი ვარ. ძალიან სასიამოვნოა.
lines.en: Hello! I am Tamari. Very nice to meet you.
Thank you. The house is very beautiful.
Thanks. Dato is a good boy, isn't he?
Yes, he's a very good boy.

note: Use formal თქვენ with in-laws and elders; they will likely address you with შენ and warm words like შვილო ('my child') right away.

title_en: What do you have, who do you have?

lines.en: Mariam, do you have a dog?

lines.ka: არა, მანქანა არ მაქვს. ველოსიპედი მაქვს.
lines.en: No, I don't have a car. I have a bicycle.
Me too. I have two brothers.

note: Georgian splits 'to have' by animacy: მყავს for people and animals, მაქვს for things — mixing them up sounds comical to native ears.

lines.en: Hello! Do you have a room?
Does the room have a balcony?

lines.ka: კი, ერთი ძაღლი გვყავს. კარგი ძაღლია.
lines.en: Yes, we have one dog. It's a good dog.

lines.ka: მე შვილი მყავს. პრობლემა არ არის?
lines.en: I have a child. Is that not a problem?

note: Georgian guesthouses are usually family-run, and hosts tend to treat guests — especially children — like their own relatives.

lines.ka: კი, ვიცი. ხინკალი აქ ძალიან გემრიელია.
lines.en: Yes, I know it. The khinkali here is very tasty.
Me too. I don't want wine today.

note: Khinkali are eaten by hand, and it's completely normal to leave the doughy top knots piled on your plate.

lines.en: Okay. Do you also want vitamins?
No, thank you. Is this medicine good?
Thanks. I also want water, a small bottle.

note: Pharmacies (აფთიაქი) are on nearly every Tbilisi corner, and ბატონო is a polite address used with everyone regardless of gender.

lines.en: Metro Rustaveli. How much does it cost?

lines.ka: რვა ლარი? რატომ? ეს ახლოს არის!

lines.en: Fine, five. When do you want to go? Now?

note: With street taxis the price is agreed before the ride starts, and a little friendly bargaining is expected.

lines.en: This is sulguni. It's very tasty.
Fine. How much do you want?

note: At the bazaar gentle haggling is part of the ritual, and vendors will happily let you taste the cheese before you buy.

lines.en: Hello, Levan! What are you doing?
Well done! How do you study?
I study online. I write and I read.
Great! I'm working a lot today.
Shall we have lunch together tomorrow?

note: ალო is the standard way to answer the phone, and ყოჩაღ is a warm 'well done!' used constantly with learners.

lines.en: I'm drawing. I'm drawing a picture.
I'm writing. I'm writing a book.
Well done! I also draw every day.

note: Georgians compliment freely and sincerely; the graceful response is a simple გმადლობ rather than deflecting the praise.

lines.en: Dato, do you usually get up early?
Yes, I get up very early. You?
I have breakfast and I work.
Do you rest in the evening?
Yes, I rest and watch TV.

note: On a marshutka (shared minibus) you pay the driver directly and small talk with strangers is perfectly normal.

lines.en: Yes, I am. It's very tasty.

note: At a supra the tamada (toastmaster) leads the toasts, and გაუმარჯოს — 'may it be victorious' — is the classic toast formula.

lines.ka: ალო, თამარ! გირეკავ და არ მპასუხობ!
lines.en: Hello, Tamar! I keep calling you and you don't answer me!
Forgive me! I'm working. What's going on?
I need help. Will you help me tomorrow?

lines.ka: რა თქმა უნდა გეხმარები! რა გინდა?
lines.en: Of course I'll help you! What do you need?
I have furniture and it's very heavy.
Okay. Zura is coming too, he'll help us.
I'll be waiting for you early tomorrow, okay?

note: Friends — especially women — often end phone calls with გკოცნი ('I kiss you'), the Georgian equivalent of 'kisses, bye'.

lines.en: Nino! Hello! Do you recognize me?

lines.ka: ვაიმე, ზურა! რა თქმა უნდა გიცნობ!
lines.en: Oh my, Zura! Of course I recognize you!
I'm so glad! How are you?
Neither do you! Do you remember Mariami?
Yes, I remember her! Where is she now?
She's here, she's waiting for us!

note: Chance street encounters in Tbilisi routinely turn into spontaneous coffee — refusing to join, even briefly, can feel cold.

lines.en: Excuse me, where is the pharmacy?

lines.ka: არა, ახლოს. ამ ქუჩაზეა, იქ, მარჯვნივ.
lines.en: No, it's close. It's on this street, over there, on the right.
Thank you! Is there also a metro here?
Yes, the metro is on the square.
I'm staying in a hotel, in Vake.

lines.ka: ვაკეში? კარგი უბანია! ავტობუსი იქ ჩერდება, მოედანზე.
lines.en: In Vake? Nice neighborhood! The bus stops there, on the square.

note: Tbilisians give directions by landmarks and metro stations rather than street numbers, and Rustaveli Avenue is the city's main axis.

lines.en: Eka, what are you doing today?
In the morning I'm going to the bazaar.

lines.ka: მერე ბანკში მივდივარ და საღამოს კინოში.
lines.en: Then I'm going to the bank, and in the evening to the cinema.
I want to go to the cinema too! What's playing?
A Georgian film. The cinema is on Rustaveli.
On the square, at eight o'clock.

note: Evening plans in Tbilisi are made casually and run late — 'at eight' comfortably stretches to quarter past.

lines.en: Levan, where are you going tomorrow?

lines.ka: მთაწმინდაზე. ბებიის სახლი ძველია, მაგრამ ლამაზი.
lines.en: On Mtatsminda. Grandma's house is old but beautiful.
Nice! My grandma lives in a village.
Are you often at your grandma's?

lines.ka: კი, ზაფხულში. ბებიის ბაღი ძალიან დიდია.
lines.en: Yes, in summer. Grandma's garden is very big.

lines.ka: კვირას მეგობრის სახლში ვართ. მოდი შენც!
lines.en: On Sunday we'll be at a friend's house. You come too!

note: Spending the summer at grandmother's village house is a cherished ritual for generations of Georgian families.

lines.en: Your sister is pretty! Where does she live?
In Kutaisi, together with her husband.
That's my father's brother, my uncle.
He lives near us, doesn't he?
Yes! My uncle's house is on our street.

note: Georgian kinship words are wonderfully precise — ბიძაჩემი fuses 'uncle' and 'my' into a single everyday word.

lines.en: Hello! How much are the tomatoes?
Hello, madam! Four lari a kilo.
Four? Over there it's three lari!
These are village tomatoes, very tasty!

lines.ka: კარგი. ორი კილო მინდა. შვიდი ლარი, კარგი?
lines.en: Fine. I want two kilos. Seven lari, okay?

lines.ka: კარგი, შვიდი ლარი. კიდევ რა გინდათ?
lines.en: Okay, seven lari. What else would you like?

lines.ka: კი, ქინძიც მაქვს და ტარხუნაც. ერთი ლარი.
lines.en: Yes, I have coriander and tarragon too. One lari.
Okay. Eight lari in total, right?

note: Vendors address women as ქალბატონო and men as ბატონო, and calling produce 'from the village' (სოფლის) is the ultimate selling point.

lines.en: Eka, do you want wine or lemonade?
I'd like a little wine, thank you.

lines.ka: ეს ბაბუის ღვინოა. ჩვენ სოფელში ვაკეთებთ.
lines.en: This is grandpa's wine. We make it in our village.
It's very tasty! How much wine do you make?
I'm learning! I love the Georgian language very much.

note: Homemade wine from the family village is a point of deep pride, and a toast to the guest is a fixed moment of every supra.
